topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printout lay crumpled on the kitchen counter, the stark genetic markers screaming accusations at me. My hands were shaking so badly I could barely hold the paper steady. I reread the results, the names listed beside my own, and a cold, hard knot tightened in my chest, spreading outward like ice. "This is a joke," I whispered, the sound raspy and brittle. I snatched the file, my knuckles white as I gripped the edges. The air in the small apartment suddenly felt thick, suffocating. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the cramped living room, my breath coming in short, sharp gasps. Every surface seemed to mock me, every corner of the room radiating judgment. I slammed my fist onto the wooden table, the impact jarring, and the resulting thud echoed loudly in the silence.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the colors of the DNA report mocking my fragile composure. It was undeniable. The data didn't lie. A visceral wave of pure fury washed over me, leaving a burning heat behind my eyes. "No," I managed to choke out, my voice dangerously low, vibrating with suppressed violence. I threw the tablet across the room; it hit the wall with a sharp crack. I didn't bother to pick it up. I began to move, a jerky, uncontrolled energy propelling me through the apartment. I kicked over a chair, the sound of splintering wood a deliberate act of defiance. I ran to the window, staring out at the indifferent streetlights, my jaw clenched so tight I could feel the muscles in my neck strain.

[story 3]

The confirmation email sat glowing on my laptop, a damning piece of evidence. I read the lineage one more time, my vision tunneling. A slow, deliberate tremor started in my hands, escalating quickly into a full-body rigidity. I dropped the laptop onto the floor, the screen going dark, but the damage was already done. I walked to the hallway mirror, my reflection staring back—a stranger contorted by a terrifying realization. I didn't scream; the sound was trapped somewhere deep in my throat, a physical pressure building behind my sternum. I began to pace again, faster this time, my footsteps heavy and purposeful on the linoleum. I stopped abruptly, turning to face the empty room, my eyes blazing with an intensity that felt almost painful to witness.
